findings.title: The three P1 resolution pathologies (ED-295/296/297) have coherent, non-interacting ratified fixes
findings.claim: The known degenerate behaviors of the interaction matrix are all closed with fixes that do not conflict: CLASH no longer stalls (resistance erodes every two exchanges so long contests resolve organically), REINFORCE cannot go negative (max(0,..) floor), and coalition dominance is an intentional parliamentary-politics design rather than an imbalance. Solo advocacy is not made pointless because erosion lifts ALL interaction types including solo CLASH, so a solo orator still resolves a contest of normal length.

locations.quote: ED-295 (CLASH stalls at median margin): Option D — audience resistance erodes per exchange. Per-exchange erosion: `Stab_resistance_t = max(0, Stab_resistance_0 − ⌊exchange_count / 2⌋)`
REINFORCE | Same genre, same orientation | Same as CLASH | max(0, (Margin − 1) + Cha modifier − Foc defence)
ED-297 (Coalition Push magnitude ~5/exchange vs solo ~0): RATIFIED as canonical design. Coalitions mechanically dominate solo advocacy by intent (parliamentary politics).

findings.impact: The resolution matrix produces sensible movement at median dice across all four interaction outcomes; no dead CLASH, no negative REINFORCE underflow, and coalition-vs-solo is a deliberate strategic axis rather than a dominant-strategy trap.

verdict.evidence_quote: **ED-295** (CLASH stalls at median margin): Option D — audience resistance erodes per exchange. Per-exchange erosion: `Stab_resistance_t = max(0, Stab_resistance_0 − ⌊exchange_count / 2⌋)`. ... Spec applies to all Interaction Types (CLASH, REINFORCE, CROSS, Coalition Push).
verdict.reasoning: All three cited quotes are locatable and in-context in params/contest.md, and the claim holds at the level it operates — ratified-design coherence of the three P1 resolution pathologies. (1) ED-295: the erosion formula is at params/contest.md:266 in the "P1 Findings — Resolved 2026-05-17 (audit ED-864)" block, and crucially the same line states "Spec applies to all Interaction Types (CLASH, REINFORCE, CROSS, Coalition Push)" — this directly substantiates the finding's load-bearing sub-claim that erosion lifts solo CLASH so a solo orator still resolves a normal-length contest. The spec corroborates: social_contest_v30.md:307 cites ED-864/ED-295 Option D and RETIRES the older ED-582 Deadlock resistance-drop as redundant precisely because the per-exchange erosion subsumes it, which is positive evidence the fixes were reconciled rather than left to collide. (2) ED-296: the REINFORCE floor is present twice — Interaction Types table at params/contest.md:91 (`max(0, (Margin − 1) + Cha modifier − Foc defence)`) and the resolved block at :267. No underflow path remains. (3) ED-297: coalition dominance is explicitly RATIFIED as intentional parliamentary design at :268, and §9.2/§10 (social_contest_v30.md:449-512) implement the coalition shared-pool consistently with that intent. The three fixes touch independent quantities (resistance decay, a movement floor, coalition pool size) and do not interact — non-conflicting as claimed. One real wrinkle, but it does NOT refute A1-1: the sim does NOT implement the ED-295 erosion. sim/personal/contest.py run_contest (lines 189-196) reads a single constant `resistance = stakes.get('resistance', RESISTANCE_DEFAULT)` and passes it unchanged into every resolve_exchange call; the loop variable `n`/`exchange_n` is passed to resolve_exchange but is never used to decay resistance (movement = max(0, margin - resistance), line 156). So there is a genuine spec-vs-sim operational gap on the very fix this finding praises. But A1-1 is scoped to whether the RATIFIED FIXES are coherent and non-interacting as design, not to whether the sim has caught up — and at that scope the claim is sound. The sim under-implementation is a distinct operational-coherence finding, not a contradiction of A1-1.
verdict.fix_assessment: Finding has empty fix (it is a strength), which is appropriate. On engine-discipline of the ED-295 fix it endorses: the erosion is a local scalar decay on the existing resistance parameter — it reuses the contest's existing resistance bucket (a flat scalar subtracted from margin) rather than bolting on new machinery, so it is discipline-consistent with the existing contest primitive. It does not reach the two-primitive core (sigma resolver / armature dot-product / Key bus), but it does not need to; resistance was always a local contest scalar. Recommend the finding (or a sibling) note that the praise is spec-level only: the ratified erosion is not yet wired into sim/personal/contest.py, so "long contests resolve organically" is currently a paper guarantee, not a simulated one.

findings.title: Sim implements a degenerate single-compare kernel — interaction types, genre/Recall/Momentum dice, strain and Concentration are all absent
findings.claim: resolve_exchange() reduces every exchange to one margin-vs-resistance comparison of two bare (PrimaryAttr×2)+History pools. There is no CLASH/REINFORCE/CROSS branch, no genre/orientation/audience bonus dice, no Recall +2D, no Momentum spend, and no Composure/Strain/Concentration/Spent bookkeeping. The pool builder only knows primary_attribute, history, wounds, and contest_fatigue. This is not the canonical kernel; it is a simplification that omits every mechanic that makes one exchange differ from another.

locations.quote:
margin = abs(a_net - b_net)
    if a_net > b_net:
        winner = 'A'
        movement = max(0, margin - resistance)

pool = (primary * 2) + history + (-1 * wounds) + fatigue_penalty
    return max(1, pool)

CLASH (same genre, opposite orientation):
... CROSS (different genres):
- No direct comparison. Each argument evaluated independently.
- Effective margin for each = floor(successes ÷ 2).

findings.impact: The sim cannot exercise the interaction matrix, the strain/Composure economy, or the Concentration/Spent action-economy — the very systems whose balance SIM-DEBT-03 claims to have confirmed. Any balance number produced by this sim describes a different game than the canonical spec.
findings.fix: Branch resolve_exchange on a declared (genre, orientation) per side to select CLASH/REINFORCE/CROSS/TIE and apply the §4 movement + strain formulas; add genre/audience/Recall/Momentum to build_argue_pool; track Composure (Cha×3) and Concentration ((3×Focus)+(2×Spirit)) per actor. Reuse the existing sigma resolver (roll_pool) for the rolls — no new primitive needed.

verdict.evidence_quote:
margin = abs(a_net - b_net)
    if a_net > b_net:
        winner = 'A'
        movement = max(0, margin - resistance)
verdict.reasoning:
The factual core is verified verbatim. In sim/personal/contest.py, resolve_exchange (lines 142-159) builds two pools via build_argue_pool, rolls each through roll_pool, and reduces the exchange to a single abs(a_net-b_net) margin-vs-resistance compare with no CLASH/REINFORCE/CROSS/TIE branch, no orientation/genre/audience/Recall/Momentum, and no Composure/Concentration/Spent. build_argue_pool (line 115: `pool = (primary * 2) + history + (-1 * wounds) + fatigue_penalty`) knows only primary_attribute, history, wounds, contest_fatigue. A repo-wide grep of sim/ for CLASH|REINFORCE|CROSS|orientation|Composure|Concentration|Spent returns zero hits in any contest-resolution path (only unrelated knots.py/thread/ matches). So the defect as a description of THIS file is sound.

But the finding is overstated on two load-bearing framings. (1) "This is not the canonical kernel" inverts the repo's source-of-truth rule. The canonical kernel IS fully specified — social_contest_v30.md §4 Step 4 gives CLASH/REINFORCE/CROSS/TIE with exact strain formulas, and params/contest.md §4 line 44 states "Interaction type derived: same style = REINFORCE, same genre opposite orientation = CLASH, different genre = CROSS" plus the full movement/strain table (lines 90-93). The spec is canon; sim/personal/contest.py is an explicitly partial implementation (its sister file tribunal.py even carries a "[PROVISIONAL — single-roll abstraction ... full multi-Exchange Contest engine is canonical Phase 5 work, deferred]" banner). The finding treats the sim's incompleteness as a canon defect rather than known sim-debt.

(2) The impact claim is REFUTED. It asserts the sim undermines "the very systems whose balance SIM-DEBT-03 claims to have confirmed." But SIM-DEBT-03 was NOT run against sim/personal/contest.py. tests/sim/sim_debt_03_04_contest_baselines.md explicitly exercises CLASH, REINFORCE, CROSS, AMPLIFY, adjudicator pools, Composure, Concentration analytically (e.g. "B — REINFORCE ... Movement=floor((1.2−1)×1.0×1.0−1)=−1. NEGATIVE for winner. F01 P1→ED-296") — it is what SURFACED ED-295/296/297 that drove the spec patches. Separately, tests/sim_framework/contest.py independently branches all four interaction types and tracks genre_bonus/audience_bonus/recall_bonus/grand_contest_recall/composure/concentration/spent/forfeit/deadlock (lines 53-101). So the interaction matrix and strain/Concentration economy ARE exercised in the test corpus; the balance confirmation does not rest on the degenerate kernel the finding cites.

Net: a real, verifiable defect (the production sim entry point is a degenerate stub that cannot exercise the spec kernel) but mis-scoped — critical severity rests on an unfounded "invalidates SIM-DEBT-03" claim and a canon-vs-sim inversion.
verdict.fix_assessment: Engine-disciplined and correct in shape. resolve_exchange should branch on declared (genre, orientation) per side to select CLASH/REINFORCE/CROSS/TIE and apply the §4 movement+strain formulas (Cha modifier max(0,floor((Cha-3)/2))*3, Focus defence floor(Foc/2)*3, all min 0), and build_argue_pool should add the genre/audience/Recall(+2D)/Momentum buckets; track Composure=Cha*3 and Concentration=(3*Focus)+(2*Spirit). The reuse claim is verified: contest.py already imports roll_pool from sim.autoload.dice_engine (line 43), so "reuse the existing sigma resolver — no new primitive" is exact, not aspirational. Two tightenings: (a) the fix text says Composure=Cha×3 — correct per spec §6/§8 — but should not echo the finding-adjacent "Cha×3" while build code elsewhere (tests/sim_framework/contest.py line 26) still uses the stale "charisma + 6"; the implementer must take the ×3 form (ED-898). (b) Concentration must use (3*Focus)+(2*Spirit) per derived_stats §14.1 / ED-902, NOT the deprecated Focus×3 — the contest.py docstring at line 51 still cites the struck "Focus × 3" form and should be corrected in the same change. (c) Rather than re-implement the matrix from scratch, the disciplined move is to lift the already-working branch logic from tests/sim_framework/contest.py into the production module so the two converge on one kernel.

findings.title: Sim omits ED-864 per-exchange resistance erosion — the very fix that resolves the CLASH stall
findings.claim: run_contest holds resistance constant for the whole contest; resolve_exchange takes a fixed resistance argument and never decrements it by ⌊exchange_count/2⌋. The canonical anti-stall mechanism (Option D erosion) is therefore not modeled, so the sim would reproduce exactly the ED-295 stall the design already fixed — and cannot validate that the fix works.

locations.quote:
for n in range(1, exchange_count + 1):
        result, track = resolve_exchange(parties, n, track, resistance, world=world, rng=rng)
First exchange uses baseline resistance; every two exchanges thereafter reduces audience resistance by 1. ... Spec applies to all Interaction Types (CLASH, REINFORCE, CROSS, Coalition Push).

findings.impact: Balance of long contests (Grand Contest = 5 exchanges; chain contests) cannot be validated; the sim under-resolves and would falsely flag stalls that the canonical erosion rule prevents.
findings.fix: In run_contest, compute resistance_t = max(0, resistance_0 − (n//2)) per exchange n and pass it into resolve_exchange, matching the ED-864 floor.

verdict.evidence_quote: for n in range(1, exchange_count + 1):\n        result, track = resolve_exchange(parties, n, track, resistance, world=world, rng=rng)
verdict.reasoning:
Both cited quotes are verbatim and in-context. In sim/personal/contest.py, run_contest reads `resistance = stakes.get('resistance', RESISTANCE_DEFAULT)` (line 190) ONCE and passes the same `resistance` into resolve_exchange on every iteration of the exchange loop (lines 195-196). resolve_exchange (lines 119-122) accepts `resistance: int` as a fixed scalar and never recomputes it; the movement math is `max(0, margin - resistance)` (lines 156, 159) — precisely the geometry ED-295 flagged as stalling at the median. So the per-exchange erosion is genuinely absent from the sim.

The canonical rule is real and authoritative, stated identically in THREE canonical loci: (1) params/contest.md "P1 Findings — Resolved 2026-05-17 (audit ED-864)" line 266 — "Stab_resistance_t = max(0, Stab_resistance_0 − ⌊exchange_count / 2⌋) ... every two exchanges thereafter reduces audience resistance by 1 ... Spec applies to all Interaction Types (CLASH, REINFORCE, CROSS, Coalition Push)"; (2) designs/scene/social_contest_v30.md §6 line 307 confirms the same floor and notes it SUBSUMES the older ED-582 Deadlock resistance-drop; (3) params/contest_extensions.md references the ED-864 resolution set. The fix it implements (Option D) is the canonical anti-stall mechanism for ED-295.

I checked whether the gap is handled elsewhere and it is NOT: grep across sim/ for erosion/exchange_count/resistance_t/ED-864/n//2 surfaces only mass-battle morale erosion and unrelated `//2` uses — nothing in the contest pipeline decrements resistance per exchange. tribunal.py is a single-roll abstraction that explicitly defers the multi-Exchange engine ("Persuasion Track tick-by-tick, Resistance per Exchange ... deferred", lines 6-11), so it does not cover this either. The omission therefore stands across the whole personal-scale contest code.

Impact is real: Grand Contest = 5 exchanges and chain contests are exactly the long-contest regime ED-295/Option D was designed to unstall; with resistance held constant the sim will under-resolve and reproduce the very median stall the design already fixed, and cannot validate SIM-DEBT-03's claim ("ED-295/297 confirmed working") which the params file marks RESOLVED. One honest caveat keeping this at major rather than critical: the default contest is 3 exchanges, where erosion only ever subtracts 1 (⌊3/2⌋=1) and only from exchange 3 onward, so short-contest behavior is mildly affected and the divergence is bounded; the validation gap bites hardest precisely at the 5-exchange/chain cases the finding names. Major is the right severity — it is a genuine spec-vs-sim divergence on a ratified canonical rule, not merely cosmetic, but it is a missing modifier rather than a structural/contract break.
verdict.fix_assessment: The proposed fix is correct and engine-disciplined. `resistance_t = max(0, resistance_0 − (n//2))` per exchange n exactly matches the canonical floor `max(0, Stab_resistance_0 − ⌊exchange_count/2⌋)` and the "first exchange uses baseline; every two thereafter −1" semantics (n=1→−0, n=2→−1, n=3→−1, n=4→−2, n=5→−2). It is a 2-line change localized to run_contest's existing loop, reuses the resistance argument resolve_exchange already takes and the existing `max(0, margin - resistance)` movement primitive — no bolt-on, no new resolver, no Key-bus touch needed since this is intra-contest state. One tightening: preserve the immutable baseline by naming it explicitly (e.g. resistance_0 = stakes.get('resistance', RESISTANCE_DEFAULT)) and compute resistance_t = max(0, resistance_0 - (n // 2)) inside the loop, so the spec's "all Interaction Types" scope is honored uniformly (the sim's resolve_exchange already applies resistance type-agnostically, so this is automatically satisfied). The fix should also note ED-895 first-to-speak/tie paths are unaffected since they do not consult resistance.

findings.title: Contest Fatigue mis-gated in sim: fires on every Decisive win, not only on Total Victory
findings.claim: run_contest grants Contest Fatigue to the loser whenever winner is 'A' or 'B' (i.e. any Decisive resolution, track ≥7 or ≤3). The canonical rule attaches Contest Fatigue ONLY to Total Victory (track ≥9 or ≤1). The sim already computes total_victory but does not use it to gate fatigue.

locations.quote:
contest_fatigue = []
    if winner == 'A':
        losing = next((p for p in parties if getattr(p, 'side', None) == 'B'), parties[1])
        contest_fatigue.append(getattr(losing, 'actor_id', 'B'))
Total Victory (Persuasion Track ≥ 9 or ≤ 1): losing primary orator gains Contest Fatigue (−1D next social roll, one instance per session, clears at next session start if unused).

findings.impact: Over-applies the −1D fatigue penalty, biasing any multi-contest or campaign-cadence simulation toward escalating disadvantage for losers and corrupting downstream social-roll balance estimates.
findings.fix: Gate the fatigue branch on the already-computed total_victory flag (only assign Contest Fatigue when total_victory is True), per §6.

verdict.evidence_quote: total_victory = (track >= PERSUASION_TOTAL_VICTORY or track <= PERSUASION_TOTAL_DEFEAT)
verdict.reasoning: Verified directly in sim/personal/contest.py. run_contest sets winner='A' when track>=PERSUASION_WIN_THRESHOLD (7) and 'B' when track<=PERSUASION_LOSS_THRESHOLD (3) — i.e. on any Decisive resolution (lines 200-205). The Contest Fatigue block (lines 238-244) fires unconditionally on `if winner == 'A'` / `elif winner == 'B'`, so it grants fatigue on EVERY Decisive win. The sim already computes the correct gate at line 207 — `total_victory = (track >= PERSUASION_TOTAL_VICTORY or track <= PERSUASION_TOTAL_DEFEAT)` with constants 9 and 1 (lines 58-59) — but uses it ONLY to populate the ContestResult.total_victory field, never to gate fatigue. Canon confirms fatigue is Total-Victory-only on two independent surfaces: designs/scene/social_contest_v30.md line 234 — "Total Victory (Persuasion Track >= 9 or <= 1): losing primary orator gains Contest Fatigue (-1D next social roll...)"; and params/contest.md line 135 Domain Echo table — "Total Victory (>=9 or <=1) | Contest Fatigue on loser (-1D next social roll)...", with the Decisive rows (Memory/Projection genre) granting NO fatigue. I checked for any downstream re-gate or compensating handler: grep for `contest_fatigue` across the whole repo returns only contest.py (one file); tribunal.py and the test suite contain no references. Nothing addresses the gap anywhere in the corpus. The finding's quoted code matches the file (the finding shows only the winner=='A' half; the elif winner=='B' half at lines 242-244 confirms the same mis-gate symmetrically). Holds exactly as described.
verdict.fix_assessment: Correct and engine-disciplined. Wrap the existing two-branch loser-identification block under `if total_victory:`, reusing the already-computed flag (which itself reuses canonical constants PERSUASION_TOTAL_VICTORY=9 / PERSUASION_TOTAL_DEFEAT=1) — no new threshold, no bolt-on. Must keep both branches so the directional loser (B when winner=='A', A when winner=='B') is still identified correctly; the finding's one-sided fix phrasing ("only assign when total_victory is True") is right but should preserve that symmetry. Severity confirmed major: Decisive outcomes (>=7/<=3) are substantially more frequent than Total Victory (>=9/<=1), so over-application is high-rate and silently biases exactly the multi-contest/campaign social-roll balance the sim exists to estimate. Not critical — sim-only, no crash — but materially corrupts the deliverable.

findings.title: Sim ties are hard-wired toward side A, breaking the first-to-speak semantics that determine tie direction
findings.claim: On equal net successes the sim always moves the track +1 toward A, with an inline admission that first-to-speak is not modeled. Canonically the tie +1 goes toward the first-to-speak HOLDER, which is rolled at exchange 1 and then transfers to the exchange winner — a state the sim does not track. This injects a systematic directional bias toward side A across all tied exchanges.

locations.quote:
winner = None
        movement = +1   # toward A by convention if no first-to-speak override
Persuasion Track moves +1 toward first-to-speak holder's position. ... Subsequent exchanges: transfers to exchange winner. On tie: stays with current holder.

findings.impact: Tied exchanges (non-trivial at TN 7 where many rolls cluster) all push toward A, giving side A a free directional edge the canon does not grant; symmetric-contest win-rate estimates are skewed.
findings.fix: Track a first_to_speak holder: initialize via an Attunement-vs-Attunement roll at exchange 1, transfer to the exchange winner, hold on tie; move the tie +1 toward that holder rather than unconditionally toward A.

verdict.evidence_quote: winner = None\n        movement = +1   # toward A by convention if no first-to-speak override
verdict.reasoning: The finding holds as described and is not addressed anywhere in the corpus. Sim: sim/personal/contest.py resolve_exchange() tie branch (lines 160-165) unconditionally sets movement = +1 with the inline admission "toward A by convention if no first-to-speak override." A grep of the entire module (only hits: lines 161-165) confirms the sim carries NO first-to-speak state at all — no Attunement-vs-Attunement roll at exchange 1, no transfer to the exchange winner, no hold-on-tie. run_contest() threads only `track` across the exchange loop (line 196), so there is no holder to read even if resolve_exchange wanted one; the comment "caller can swap by side label" is aspirational — nothing in the signature or body consumes such an override. Canon is exactly what the finding asserts. designs/scene/social_contest_v30.md §4 TIE (line 176): "Persuasion Track moves +1 toward first-to-speak holder's position." §5 FIRST TO SPEAK (lines 209-211): exchange 1 ROLLED (both orators roll Attunement, TN 7, Ob 1; higher net acts last), "Subsequent exchanges: transfers to exchange winner. On tie: stays with current holder." params/contest.md independently confirms in two places: §First to Speak (lines 66-67) and the TIE row of the interaction table (line 93: "track +1 toward first-to-speak holder"), plus PP-236 (line 174). So the holder is a genuine rolled-then-transferred state variable, and the sim collapses it to a constant bias toward side A across every tied exchange. The directional-bias impact is real for symmetric-contest win-rate estimates (the sim's purpose). I rate it major rather than critical because the per-tie magnitude is only ±1 on a 0-10 track that must reach ≥7/≤3, and exact net ties are a minority of exchanges at TN 7 — it distorts balance numbers but does not corrupt canon or the engine; it is a sim-fidelity defect localized to one branch.
verdict.fix_assessment: Engine-disciplined and correct, with one structural caveat the finding implies but should be explicit about: the holder must be threaded as state through run_contest()'s exchange loop, because resolve_exchange() currently has no cross-exchange memory (it only receives/returns `track`). Concretely: (1) initialize a first_to_speak holder before exchange 1 via an Attunement-vs-Attunement roll — reuse the existing roll_pool(...).net path (TN 7, Ob 1) exactly as combat initiative does, not the Argue pool — with the canonical tiebreaks (higher Attunement stat, else neutral assignment); (2) pass the holder into resolve_exchange and, on a decisive exchange, transfer it to the exchange winner; on a tie, hold it; (3) move the tie's +1 toward the held holder's side rather than unconditionally +1. This reuses the sigma/net resolver and existing side-label plumbing with no new primitive and no bolt-on — it just makes resolve_exchange stateful in the holder the way run_contest is already stateful in track. Recommend adding the holder to ExchangeResult for auditability, mirroring how track_movement is already surfaced.

findings.title: SIM-DEBT-03 marked RESOLVED while the live sim models none of the redesigned mechanics
findings.claim: params/contest.md closes SIM-DEBT-03 ('Full re-sim under two-genre system') as confirmed via historical precedent, yet the actual sim/personal/contest.py exercises neither the two-genre interaction matrix nor the strain/Concentration economy it is supposed to have re-simmed. The closure rests on 'historical precedent' rather than a run of the current kernel, so the corpus presents un-validated balance as validated.

locations.quote: SIM-DEBT-03 | Full re-sim under two-genre system. | RESOLVED — ED-295/297 confirmed working via historical precedent.
SIM-DEBT-03 | Full re-simulation under two-genre system with integer bonus dice. All prior SIM-D baselines invalidated.
resolve_exchange: §4 exchange resolution (pool roll, margin vs resistance, Persuasion Track update)

findings.impact: Decision-makers may treat the resolution kernel as balance-verified when the only executable model is a stripped abstraction; the two debt entries even disagree on status (params=RESOLVED, spec §12=open), an internal-consistency seam.
findings.fix: Either reopen SIM-DEBT-03 until the sim implements the canonical kernel (findings A1-2..A1-5), or re-label the closure as 'analytic/precedent-only, not sim-confirmed' and reconcile the spec §12 entry to match params.

verdict.evidence_quote: movement = max(0, margin - resistance)
verdict.reasoning:
The core claim holds on independent inspection. All three cited quotes are verbatim: params/contest.md line 167 closes SIM-DEBT-03 "RESOLVED — ED-295/297 confirmed working via historical precedent"; social_contest_v30.md line 610 lists SIM-DEBT-03 as "Full re-simulation under two-genre system with integer bonus dice. All prior SIM-D baselines invalidated"; and the contest.py docstring describes only the stripped "pool roll, margin vs resistance, Persuasion Track update" pipeline.

I read resolve_exchange (contest.py:119-175) and confirmed the live kernel is a pure margin abstraction: winner movement = max(0, margin - resistance), with no notion of genre (Memory/Projection), orientation (Direct/Indirect), or interaction type. params/contest.md by contrast canonicalizes a four-way interaction matrix (CLASH/REINFORCE/CROSS/TIE, lines 88-93) each with distinct movement AND strain formulas, plus genre/orientation bonus dice (+1D/+2D, lines 45-50). None of this exists in the sim. CONCENTRATION_MULTIPLIER is declared (line 52) but a grep shows it is never referenced in any computation — the "strain/Concentration economy" the re-sim was meant to validate is entirely absent. Greps for strain|genre|CLASH|REINFORCE|CROSS in contest.py return nothing but the dead constant.

The closure provenance is precedent, not a kernel run: archives/session/session_log_archive_part_7.md:122 records "ED-295/297 resolved via historical precedent." Worse, the very ED the closure leans on is still live: canon/editorial_ledger.jsonl:183 carries ED-295 as status "provisional" with description "ED-295 (OPEN, P1-BLOCKER): CLASH stalls at median" — the closure cites as "confirmed working" a fix the ledger still flags as an open P1 blocker. git log on contest.py shows only scaffold + infill commits (ce4c0663, 80b4eb7e); no commit implemented the redesigned kernel. So the corpus does present un-validated balance as validated. The finding is not already-handled — no reconciliation commit exists.

One sub-claim is overstated: the impact says the two entries "disagree on status (params=RESOLVED, spec §12=open)." The spec §12 table (lines 607-611) has NO status column at all — it lists SIM-DEBT-03 as a bare debt with no status field, so it does not literally assert "open." The real seam is that params marks it RESOLVED while the spec still tables it as live untracked debt and the sim implements none of it — an un-reconciled-presentation problem, not a literal status contradiction. This nuance does not undermine the primary claim. Severity stays major: a decision-maker reading params would reasonably treat the resolution kernel as balance-verified when the only executable model is a stripped abstraction and the cited supporting ED is an open P1 blocker.
verdict.fix_assessment: Engine-disciplined and correct, with a tightening. The proposed fix (reopen SIM-DEBT-03 until the sim implements the canonical kernel of findings A1-2..A1-5, OR relabel the closure as "analytic/precedent-only, not sim-confirmed" and reconcile spec §12 to params) is a documentation-integrity correction that reuses the existing simulation-debt ledger machinery — no bolt-on mechanic. Tighten the second branch: do not just relabel, also drop or correct the "ED-295/297 confirmed working" citation, since ED-295 is still provisional/OPEN-P1-BLOCKER in editorial_ledger.jsonl:183 — citing an open blocker as confirmation is itself the integrity defect. Preferred branch is reopen: until resolve_exchange models the CLASH/REINFORCE/CROSS/TIE matrix and the strain/Concentration economy, the precedent-based closure cannot stand. Also reconcile the disagreement-on-status framing to "spec tables it as live debt; params marks RESOLVED" rather than asserting spec says "open."

findings.title: Erosion-vs-Deadlock double-count is retired only in prose; no regression test guards against reintroduction
findings.claim: The §6.3 Deadlock resistance drop is declared redundant-and-retired because ED-864 erosion already lowers resistance, but both rules still co-exist textually in the same section, and the chain hard-stops are kept. With two resistance-lowering mechanisms documented adjacent to each other and no executable kernel enforcing only one, a future implementer can re-apply both and double-erode resistance.

locations.quote: the Deadlock resistance-drop below is redundant and retired ... If two consecutive exchanges in a chain contest produce zero track movement ... Resistance for both sides drops by 1 (minimum 0) for the remainder of this contest.
every two exchanges thereafter reduces audience resistance by 1

findings.impact: Low at the canonical level (prose marks the Deadlock drop dead), but a real coherence trap for the eventual sim/Godot implementation: applying both yields resistance collapsing too fast and contests resolving on noise.
findings.fix: Strike the Deadlock resistance-drop bullet from §6.3 outright (leaving only the chain hard-stop terminators) so exactly one resistance-erosion rule survives in text, and assert single-application in the kernel once A1-3 lands.

verdict.evidence_quote: **Resistance stall-break (ED-582 — partially subsumed, ED-896 / D-9):** [The per-exchange resistance erosion (ED-864 / ED-295 Option D: Stab_resistance_t = max(0, Stab_resistance_0 − ⌊exchange_count ÷ 2⌋), all contests) already lowers resistance every two exchanges, so the Deadlock resistance-drop below is redundant and retired. ... ] If two consecutive exchanges in a chain contest produce zero track movement (both sides' margins ≤ resistance), the contest enters Deadlock. In Deadlock:\n- Resistance for both sides drops by 1 (minimum 0) for the remainder of this contest.
verdict.reasoning: The quotes are accurate and in context. social_contest_v30.md §6.3 line 307 declares the Deadlock resistance-drop "redundant and retired" yet the operative bullet ("Resistance for both sides drops by 1 (minimum 0)…", line 308) and its third-zero-movement follow-on (line 309) remain verbatim in the same section; params/contest.md line 266 (P1 Resolved 2026-05-17) independently documents the ED-864 per-exchange erosion that subsumes it. So two resistance-lowering rules genuinely co-exist in text, separated only by a prose retirement marker. The claim that "no executable kernel enforces only one" is not just true — it is stronger than stated: sim/personal/contest.py applies NEITHER. resolve_exchange (lines 119-175) takes a static `resistance` and computes movement = max(0, margin - resistance); run_contest (lines 195-196) passes the SAME resistance every exchange with no ⌊exchange_count÷2⌋ decrement and no Deadlock path. tribunal.py is an explicitly provisional single-roll abstraction that never ticks resistance per-exchange. No regression guard exists: tests/valoria/ holds only CI-meta checks (sim-fabrication / supersession / vetting), and coverage_matrix_archive.md's contest.py entry verifies only pool/track/tie/Compromise basics, not erosion. The finding is thus correct that the retirement lives only in prose and is unguarded. Severity stays minor: the inline retirement marker is reasonably explicit, so a careful implementer would not re-apply both, and the impact is purely forward-looking (no live double-count today — if anything the live sim under-erodes). The deliberation critique (findings.json line 459) quotes the same Deadlock text but from a churn-design angle, so A1-7 is a genuinely distinct operational-coherence observation, not a duplicate.
verdict.fix_assessment: Directionally engine-disciplined but mis-frames current sim state and should be tightened. Striking the §6.3 resistance-drop bullet (leaving only the chain hard-stop terminators: third-zero-movement → Compromise, max-chain-3 cold equilibrium) is correct and reuses no new machinery. However "assert single-application in the kernel once A1-3 lands" presumes the kernel already applies one erosion; it applies zero. The disciplined fix is: (1) strike the bullet, AND (2) implement the canonical ED-864 formula Stab_resistance_t = max(0, Stab_resistance_0 − ⌊exchange_count÷2⌋) inside the existing run_contest loop by decrementing the already-present `resistance` parameter per exchange — a single in-place edit to the static value, no bolt-on, no new resolver — with a unit test asserting resistance follows that one formula and the Deadlock drop is never separately applied. That keeps exactly one erosion path in both text and code.

findings.title: §6.3 Deadlock block contradicts its own retirement note — terminator keys off a retired step
findings.claim: The ED-896 header inside §6.3 declares the per-exchange erosion makes the Deadlock resistance-drop below redundant and retired, but the two live bullets immediately below still print the resistance-drop as an active rule, and the chain hard-stop (which the header says remains in force) is defined as firing after the Resistance drop — i.e., the surviving terminator is anchored to the step the same paragraph just retired. A GM reading the live bullets applies the drop; a GM reading the header doesn't, and the third-zero-movement to Compromise trigger becomes ambiguous about when it fires.
findings.fix: Apply D-9's recommended fold cleanly: delete the resistance-drop bullet and re-anchor the terminator to raw zero-movement counts — third consecutive zero-movement exchange in a chain contest to immediate Compromise at current track — so the hard-stop no longer references a retired step. No new primitive; this is the existing track/clock bucket.
findings.impact: A live-resolution state-machine transition (Deadlock to forced Compromise) is gated on a step the spec says is retired. Two GMs reach different track outcomes for the same chain contest; the prior 2026-06-09 audit flagged the duplication (P3-15/D-9) but the half-applied edit has produced a worse state than either clean option.

locations.quote: the Deadlock resistance-drop below is redundant and retired. The chain hard-stop rules (third consecutive zero-movement → Compromise; max-chain-3 cold equilibrium) remain in force

- Resistance for both sides drops by 1 (minimum 0) for the remainder of this contest.
- If a third consecutive zero-movement exchange occurs after the Resistance drop, the contest ends immediately as a Compromise at the current track position.

verdict.evidence_quote: **Resistance stall-break (ED-582 — partially subsumed, ED-896 / D-9):** [... the Deadlock resistance-drop below is redundant and retired. The chain hard-stop rules (third consecutive zero-movement → Compromise; max-chain-3 cold equilibrium) remain in force as the chain-contest terminator.] If two consecutive exchanges in a chain contest produce zero track movement (both sides' margins ≤ resistance), the contest enters Deadlock. In Deadlock:\n- Resistance for both sides drops by 1 (minimum 0) for the remainder of this contest.\n- If a third consecutive zero-movement exchange occurs after the Resistance drop, the contest ends immediately as a Compromise at the current track position.
verdict.reasoning: The finding holds as described. In designs/scene/social_contest_v30.md §6.3 the contradiction is co-located in a single block (lines 307-309): the ED-896 header (line 307) declares "the Deadlock resistance-drop below is redundant and retired" while keeping the hard-stop terminator "in force," yet bullet 308 still prints the resistance-drop as an active rule and bullet 309 anchors the surviving terminator to "after the Resistance drop" — i.e., the kept terminator is gated on the step the same paragraph retired. I checked whether the gap is resolved elsewhere and it is not: (a) params/contest.md only records the upstream erosion (ED-295 Option D, line 266) and an ED-896 note that the stale 2026-04-04 block is obsolete — it does not re-anchor the §6.3 terminator or delete the drop; (b) sim/personal/contest.py does NOT implement either per-exchange erosion or the Deadlock/zero-movement terminator at all — resolve_exchange (line 156) uses a fixed resistance passed through stakes, so the sim is silent and cannot disambiguate; (c) no later editorial note in social_contest_v30.md supersedes §6.3 (only the one block references Deadlock/ED-582). The prior 2026-06-09 audit corroborates the lineage: ANALYSIS.md P3-15 (line 203) flags ED-582 Deadlock vs ED-864 erosion as duplicative, and D-9 (line 225) recommends "fold — erosion subsumes the trigger." The current spec is the half-applied result of that recommendation: header folds, bullets do not. Two GMs genuinely diverge on whether the third-zero trigger counts from Deadlock entry or only after applying a drop the header says no longer happens. I hold severity at major (not critical) because the underlying terminator still fires on raw zero-movement counts under either reading once the dangling "after the Resistance drop" clause is ignored — it is a live-resolution sequencing ambiguity from an incomplete edit, not an unrecoverable state-machine break, but the divergent track outcome for the same chain contest clears the major bar.
verdict.fix_assessment: Fix is engine-disciplined and correct. Deleting the resistance-drop bullet and re-anchoring the terminator to raw zero-movement counts (third consecutive zero-movement exchange in a chain contest → immediate Compromise at current track) reuses the existing track/clock bucket, introduces no new primitive, and matches D-9's ratified fold recommendation. Recommend one tightening: also reconcile the header parenthetical, which still names the resistance-drop as the thing being retired — after deletion the header should read as already-clean (erosion subsumes; terminator counts raw zero-movement) so no dangling reference to a deleted bullet remains. The intro sentence's Deadlock-entry definition (two consecutive zero-movement) is independent of the drop and can stand or be folded into the single terminator count for clarity.

findings.title: Heresy 'Insufficient Evidence' suspend-state has a resume condition with no defined satisfier
findings.claim: The Verdict phase can return Insufficient Evidence (Investigation suspended; may resume if Evidence Track reaches 3 within 4 seasons), and the Excommunication Tribunal prereq is Evidence Track >= 3 on target from prior HI. But while the Investigation is suspended there is no defined scene or actor that advances the Inquisitor's prosecutorial Evidence Track: the only Evidence Track driver in the corpus is fieldwork investigation actions (fieldwork_investigation §4.1), and §7.3.4 frames Evidence-gathering as the target's counter-evidence activity. The suspend-state's sole exit-to-resume is therefore conditioned on a value-change with no specified producer.
findings.fix: Name the producer: add an Inquisitor-driven Evidence action to the suspended state (reuse the §7.3 Interrogation/Mandatory Zoom-In scene as a once-per-season prosecutorial fieldwork roll filling the same Evidence Track clock), or collapse Insufficient Evidence into a timed Acquittal-by-default mirroring the Inquisitor-death row. Either reuses the existing clock bucket — no new subsystem.
findings.impact: A suspended Heresy Investigation can sit for up to 4 seasons with a resume condition no game actor is empowered to satisfy, then closes by timeout — making Insufficient Evidence functionally identical to Acquittal but without saying so. The escalation path HI to Tribunal advertised by the prereq is dead from this branch.

locations.quote: Insufficient Evidence (Investigation suspended; may resume if Evidence Track reaches 3 within 4 seasons)
Evidence Track ≥ 3 on target from prior HI
Each investigation has an **Evidence Track** — a progress clock that fills as the investigator gathers pieces.

verdict.evidence_quote: Insufficient Evidence (Investigation suspended; may resume if Evidence Track reaches 3 within 4 seasons)
verdict.fix_assessment: Engine-disciplined and correct. Both proposed options reuse existing buckets with no bolt-on: (a) attach the already-specified §7.3 Mandatory Zoom-In Interrogation as a once-per-season prosecutorial action that fills the same fieldwork Evidence Track clock (fieldwork §4.2 degree→progress table is the existing producer), or (b) collapse Insufficient Evidence into a timed Acquittal-by-default that literally mirrors the §7.3.2 Inquisitor-death/demotion rows already in the doc ("After 2 seasons without reassignment: Investigation closes; target may invoke Acquittal-by-default"). Option (b) is the cleaner of the two — it is a copy of an adjacent, already-ratified closure row, so it adds nothing the engine doesn't run. One refinement: if option (a) is chosen, specify that the prosecutorial Interrogation produces a Verified/Documentary-tagged Evidence increment (per fieldwork §4.3) and inherits the Church investigatory bonus (fieldwork §4.0: "+1D Investigate, +2 Ob for targets in Church territory with Inquisitor"), so the new producer is consistent with the existing institutional-investigation tags rather than a free clock-tick.
verdict.reasoning: The gap holds. Both load-bearing quotes are verbatim: §7.3.1 Verdict row (social_contest_v30.md:377) reads exactly "Insufficient Evidence (Investigation suspended; may resume if Evidence Track reaches 3 within 4 seasons)", and the §7.1 prereq (social_contest_v30.md:527) reads "Evidence Track ≥ 3 on target from prior HI". The clock-mismatch is real: the entire Heresy Investigation lifecycle (§7.3.1 Initiation/Investigation Proper/Verdict) runs on a PERSUASION Track advanced by Interrogation scenes ("asymmetric Persuasion Track, Ob 2 vs target framework" / "Conclusive Persuasion Track exchange") — it never touches the Evidence Track — yet the suspend-state's sole exit is conditioned on the fieldwork EVIDENCE Track. The general Evidence Track producer is fieldwork investigation actions only (fieldwork_investigation.md §4.1 "a progress clock that fills as the investigator gathers pieces", §4.2 action/degree table), plus a Contest-Appraise feed (scale_transitions §F-TRANS-10: "Appraise success in Contest: +1 Evidence Track progress"). §7.3.4 "Player Strategic Implications" frames Evidence-gathering in this window as the TARGET's defensive activity ("Acquire counter-Evidence (fieldwork on the original accusation...)"), confirming no Inquisitor-side prosecutorial driver is attached to the suspended state. I checked the one corpus location that comes close: arc_expansion_v30.md:256 (Olafsson Arc A) advances an Evidence Track "+2/season automatically from Inquisition resources" — but this is a single named-NPC arc passive, not a general rule, and at Evidence 8 it GENERATES A NEW Priority-2 Investigation rather than satisfying a suspended Verdict-state's resume-at-3, so it does not close the branch. The sim confirms the loop is unmodeled: tribunal.py:75-78 explicitly treats the Evidence-Track gate as "a personal-scale concept not yet ported" and substitutes the L+CI gate. Severity correction major→minor: the defect is contained to one branch of one asymmetric proceeding, and the corpus already holds a structurally identical, fully-specified timeout pattern (the §7.3.2 Acquittal-by-default rows) that the suspend-state silently duplicates — so it is a single-branch dead-end with an obvious in-corpus collapse target, not a system-wide coherence break. The finding's own characterization ("functionally identical to Acquittal but without saying so") is accurate and is exactly why minor, not major, is the honest tier.

findings.title: Succession Contest eligibility/adjudication conflicts with its own canonical Crown instance
findings.claim: §7.2 states cross-faction claimants require Mandate >= 3 in their home faction plus a structural claim mechanism (Deed-Claim, Bloodline, institutional precedent) and sets the Crown adjudicator as inner circle by majority Disposition. The canonical Crown instance it cites, baralta_crown_claim §2, instead opens the contest to All major factions with Mandate >= 3 may stake a claim (no separate structural-claim gate for general claimants) and resolves it as a multi-faction contest roll with positioned-claim bonuses (Hafenmark +2D), not an inner-circle Disposition vote. The general framework and the instance it is supposedly generalized from describe two different eligibility tests and two different adjudication models.
findings.fix: Reconcile by making §7.2 cite baralta_crown_claim as the authoritative Crown adjudicator override (multi-faction Mandate-weighted contest with positioned-claim bonuses) and reserve inner-circle by majority Disposition to non-Crown factions, or hoist the structural-claim gate into baralta_crown_claim §2. Single source-of-truth pin; no mechanical invention.
findings.impact: For the one fully-worked succession case in the corpus, a GM gets contradictory eligibility and adjudication rules depending on which doc they open; the §7.2 inner circle by majority Disposition adjudicator cannot reproduce the Hafenmark-wins outcome baralta_crown_claim is built to deliver.

locations.quote: Cross-faction challengers (e.g., Hafenmark claiming Crown per baralta_crown_claim_v30 §2) require Mandate ≥ 3 in their home faction plus a structural claim mechanism
All major factions with Mandate ≥ 3 may stake a claim.

verdict.evidence_quote: **Adjudicator:** the faction's institutional body (Crown: inner circle by majority Disposition; Hafenmark: Parliament by Mandate-weighted vote; ...). vs. baralta_crown_claim §2: "Each claiming faction rolls their pool vs Ob 3 (TN 7). Highest net successes wins."
verdict.reasoning: Both cited quotes verify verbatim. social_contest_v30.md §7.2 line 330: cross-faction challengers "require Mandate ≥ 3 in their home faction plus a structural claim mechanism (Deed-Claim, Bloodline, institutional precedent)" and line 333 sets the Crown adjudicator as "inner circle by majority Disposition." baralta_crown_claim_v30.md §2 line 33: "All major factions with Mandate ≥ 3 may stake a claim" with resolution at line 41 "Each claiming faction rolls their pool vs Ob 3 (TN 7). Highest net successes wins," plus the Hafenmark +2D positioned-claim bonus (line 54). Two genuine conflicts confirmed: (1) ELIGIBILITY — §7.2 imposes a separate structural-claim gate on cross-faction claimants; the instance gates purely on Mandate ≥ 3 (its candidate table has a "Claim Basis" column but it is descriptive flavor per candidate, not a required separate eligibility test). (2) ADJUDICATION — §7.2 Crown = an inner-circle majority-Disposition vote among Crown NPCs; the instance = a multi-faction pool roll vs Ob 3 with positioned-claim dice. These are categorically different resolution mechanics. The finding's impact note is mechanically correct: a Crown inner-circle Disposition vote cannot reproduce the Hafenmark-wins outcome the instance is built to deliver — Hafenmark is cross-faction, a Crown inner circle voting by its own Disposition would not install an outside claimant, and the +2D positioned-claim bonus has no slot in a Disposition vote. Is it already-handled? Partly addressed-but-not-resolved: the ED-665 editorial note (line 363) states "baralta_crown_claim §2 remains the canonical Crown instance; this section provides the generalizable framework for all factions" — so the docs are AWARE of the relationship, but the note asserts both are authoritative without pinning precedence on the two contradicting fields. That is not a reconciliation; it is the seam itself. A full-corpus grep confirms the "inner circle by majority Disposition" Crown adjudicator appears ONLY in §7.2 (and the prior deliberation critique that quotes it) — it is never reconciled with the instance's roll-based model anywhere. Severity held at major: it is the single fully-worked succession case in the corpus and the adjudication-model conflict is total, not cosmetic; a GM gets contradictory eligibility AND adjudication depending on which doc they open. Not critical because both docs are individually runnable and nothing is engine-breaking.
verdict.fix_assessment: Engine-disciplined. The fix is a single-source-of-truth pin / precedence declaration, not mechanical invention: it reuses the existing Grand Contest infrastructure (§3–§5) the instance already rides on and the instance's existing multi-faction pool-roll-vs-Ob with the positioned-claim +2D. The cleaner of the two offered options is to pin baralta_crown_claim §2 as the authoritative Crown adjudicator override (multi-faction Mandate-weighted contest with positioned-claim bonuses) and reserve "inner circle by majority Disposition" to non-Crown factions, since the instance is the dated, simulated, worked mechanic and §7.2 is the later generalization. Recommend also reconciling the eligibility gate: either hoist the structural-claim requirement into the instance OR explicitly note the instance's Mandate-≥3-only gate governs the Crown case (the candidate-table Claim Basis column already supplies the per-claimant structural rationale informally). No bolt-on; reuses the sigma/pool resolver and existing buckets.

findings.title: Heresy Investigation lifecycle is a complete, exit-closed state machine with matching authority gates
findings.claim: §7.3 enumerates 8 distinct closure conditions (Inquisitor death/demotion/reassignment, target death/protection/conversion/defection, acquittal) each with explicit effect and rationale, plus a multiple-Inquisitor/cross-jurisdiction handling rule — no state lacks an exit. The initiation-authority gate (rank >= 2 to non-leadership; rank >= 3 to Standing 4+) matches the Inquisitor rank ladder in faction_politics exactly, so the entry condition is consistent across docs.
findings.impact: The most complex lifecycle in the contest system (4-6 season multi-phase proceeding feeding the Tribunal) is the most coherent: closure is total, the Mandatory Zoom-In trigger in scale_transitions §4.3.2 has a defined lifecycle behind it, and the entry gate does not drift from the rank ladder. This is the model the other sub-systems should match.

locations.quote: an Inquisitor at rank ≥ 2 may **initiate** Heresy Proceedings against non-leadership NPCs/PCs; rank ≥ 3 may target Standing 4+ NPCs/PCs
May initiate Heresy Proceedings against non-leadership NPCs

verdict.evidence_quote: | 3 | Senior Inquisitor | 5 closed cases at Inquisitor rank; one case involved a Standing 4+ target | Regional Inquisitorial authority; may initiate Heresy Proceedings against Standing 4 targets with Cardinal authorization |
verdict.reasoning:
The structural half of the finding is SOUND and independently verified. designs/scene/social_contest_v30.md §7.3.2 (lines 385-394) does enumerate exactly 8 closure rows — Inquisitor death, Inquisitor demoted/excommunicated, Inquisitor reassigned, target death, target faction protection (Stay), Acquittal by Verdict, target faction conversion to Church, target defection — each with explicit Effect + Rationale columns, plus §7.3.3 multiple-Inquisitor/cross-jurisdiction handling (lines 396-403). Every state has an eventual exit: the two suspend-only states (reassignment → "closes per Inquisitor death rule" after 2 seasons; Parliamentary Stay → bounded by CI rising to 55) and the Insufficient-Evidence verdict (resumes only if Evidence Track hits 3 within 4 seasons) all terminate. The Mandatory Zoom-In trigger it backs is real: scale_transitions_v30.md §4.3.2 line 132 "Heresy Investigation Target ... an active Heresy Investigation." So "complete, exit-closed state machine" is fair.

The finding is OVERSTATED on its OTHER headline asset — the entry-gate "matches the Inquisitor rank ladder in faction_politics exactly ... does not drift." It does drift, twice. (1) faction_politics_v30.md §2.3 rank 3 (line 468) gates Standing-4 targeting on "with Cardinal authorization"; social_contest §7.3 (line 369) drops that qualifier entirely — it reads as an unconditional rank-3 power. (2) social_contest says "rank ≥ 3 may target Standing 4+"; the ladder says "Standing 4" (not 4+), and faction_politics §1.4 (lines 762, 774) shows Standing 5+/6 targeting is a SEPARATE higher gate (Bishop's Delegate; or Olafsson "against Standing 6 targets"), so the "+" over-claims the ceiling. Rank-2/non-leadership does match exactly (line 467). So "matches exactly" is true for one of two gate clauses and loose-to-wrong for the other.

Note for context (not the basis of the verdict, since the finding scopes itself to lifecycle coherence not Key wiring): the lifecycle is unkeyed. key_type_registry_v30.md line 705 defines scene.investigation_resolved {finding: exonerated|guilty|inconclusive} which cleanly maps to the §7.3 Verdict trichotomy, yet §7.3 never emits it and sim/personal/contest.py has zero heresy/investigation handling — matching the distillation report's "rich unkeyed state" MEDIUM flag (distillation_coherence_report.md line 165). This undercuts the impact line "the model the other sub-systems should match": it is well-specified prose but not engine-wired.

Severity stays minor — it is a strength entry; the asset is genuine, the over-claim is on a sub-clause, not a fabricated mechanic.
verdict.fix_assessment: No fix is proposed (it is a strength). The right corrective is engine-disciplined and small: (a) align social_contest §7.3 line 369 to the ladder by restoring "with Cardinal authorization" on the rank-3/Standing-4 clause and dropping the loose "4+" (or citing §1.4 for the higher ceilings) so the entry gate truly does not drift; (b) wire the Verdict outcomes to emit the existing scene.investigation_resolved Key (reusing the registry type at key_type_registry_v30.md line 705, finding ∈ {exonerated|guilty|inconclusive}) rather than leaving closure as inert prose. Both reuse existing primitives (the rank ladder and the Key bus) — no bolt-on.

findings.title: PROVISIONAL Panel adjudicator is carried as live-resolution canon in params despite spec marking it provisional
findings.claim: The spec consistently flags the Panel adjudicator as undesigned (§2 panel mechanics not yet designed. Use Expert Judge as provisional; §3 [PROVISIONAL — ED-137 ... Not fully specified.]). But params/contest.md presents the Panel pool row with the note [PROVISIONAL — pre-ledger, accepted as canonical per 2026-04-26 audit] — i.e., the same provisional content has been promoted to canonical in the params layer that GMs treat as the live formula sheet. A provisional state is being treated as canonical in live resolution, which is exactly the lens's prohibited condition.
findings.fix: Either close ED-137 by adopting the validated Panel model the distillation/comprehensive audits report already exists (member aggregation) and dropping both PROVISIONAL tags, or strike the params accepted as canonical clause so both docs agree Panel == Expert-Judge-stopgap until designed. No new primitive.
findings.impact: A multi-judge proceeding (the deliberating-panel case the system explicitly names) has no deliberation mechanics yet resolves identically to a single Expert Judge, with the two governing docs disagreeing on whether that's provisional or canonical. Low blast radius today because few proceedings invoke Panel, but it is a provisional-treated-as-canonical seam the prior audit also flagged (P3-16).

locations.quote: Panel | Cognition | [PROVISIONAL — ED-137: panel mechanics not yet designed; use Expert Judge until designed. Not fully specified.]
Argue (Panel) | (Cognition × 2) + History bonus | 7 | [PROVISIONAL — pre-ledger, accepted as canonical per 2026-04-26 audit]

verdict.evidence_quote: P3-16 — Convergence, not defect: ED-137 Panel. Canon's Panel adjudicator is provisional ("use Expert Judge"); the groundup engine has a built, validated Panel model (member character / discipline / learned-hostile aggregation). Propagation closes ED-137 for free.
verdict.reasoning: Both cited quotes are verbatim-accurate. Spec social_contest_v30.md L37 and L109 mark Panel as undesigned ("panel mechanics not yet designed; use Expert Judge"); params/contest.md L22 carries the identical Argue(Panel) = Cog×2 row tagged "[PROVISIONAL — pre-ledger, accepted as canonical per 2026-04-26 audit]". So the two governing docs do disagree on whether the same Panel content is provisional-stopgap or accepted-canonical, and a Panel proceeding resolves identically to a single Expert Judge with no deliberation. That is a real cross-doc seam and is NOT closed anywhere: I grepped the distillation report, sim/personal/contest.py, and sim/personal/tribunal.py for Panel/ED-137 — no matches. The seam is corroborated, not refuted, by the prior comprehensive audit, which flags it as P3-16 (designs/audit/2026-06-09-social-contest-comprehensive/ANALYSIS.md L205). One nuance that keeps this from being sharper: the editorial ledger (canon/editorial_ledger.jsonl L137) shows ED-137 deliberately split — "Expert Judge (Cognition-primary) confirmed provisional. Full panel mechanics deferred to Contest System v3" — so the params "accepted as canonical" tag plausibly refers to the pool-formula row while the spec "provisional" refers to the deliberation mechanics. The disagreement is thus a tag/scope mismatch rather than a hard behavioral contradiction, but it still leaves the live formula sheet and the spec stating different things about the same row, which is the lens's prohibited condition. Holds as a minor defect.
verdict.fix_assessment: Engine-disciplined and points at real code, not a bolt-on. The fix's premise — that a "validated Panel model (member aggregation) already exists" — is verified: contract.py:37-51 in the groundup engine defines a frozen Panel dataclass that aggregates members (learned/hostile by 2*sum>len majority, discipline and ethos/pathos/logos character by mean), exposing the same interface as a single Adjudicator. P3-16 and D-1 of the comprehensive audit already schedule this groundup substrate as the consolidation target that "closes ED-137 for free." So "adopt the validated Panel model and drop both PROVISIONAL tags" reuses existing buckets and the planned substrate; no new primitive. The simpler alternative branch (strike the params "accepted as canonical" clause so both docs say Panel == Expert-Judge stopgap until designed) is also valid and lower-effort. Both branches are sound; recommend the alignment-only branch unless the groundup consolidation rewrite (D-1) is being executed in the same pass. Note for the adopt branch: contract.py:43-44 has a known LOW edge (even-split panel with one learned member reads as not-learned, per the groundup FULL_AUDIT §14) that should be documented/decided before promotion.

findings.title: §7.1/§10.1 placed after §12 split the Tribunal/Stay counter-play loop from its references
findings.claim: §7.1 (Excommunication Tribunal) and §10.1 (Parliamentary Stay) are physically placed after §12 (Open Items), and §10.1 is the documented strategic counter for §7.1 (See §10.1 Parliamentary Stay. Filing can be prevented while CI < 55.). A GM reading linearly hits the §7.3 closure table's forward-references to §7.1 and §10.1 (and the §7.1 counter pointing to §10.1) hundreds of lines before either section appears, after the doc has apparently ended at §12. Pure ordering, but it directly degrades usability of the most cross-referenced lifecycle counter-play.
findings.fix: Relocate §7.1 and §10.1 to sit with §7 (asymmetric proceedings) where they are referenced, ahead of §12. Documentation move only; no mechanical change.
findings.impact: The Tribunal/Stay counter-play loop — the system's central political-defense mechanism — is split across the document with the resolver appearing after the Open-Items dump, making the lifecycle hard to trace at the table even though the rules themselves cohere.

locations.quote: *The correct strategic counter is preventing the filing, not defending at Tribunal. See §10.1 Parliamentary Stay. Filing can be prevented while CI < 55.*
If target's faction successfully passes a Parliamentary Stay (per §10.1) AND CI < 55: Investigation suspended for 1 season per Stay.

verdict.evidence_quote: ## §11 HYBRID CONTEST [line 516] ... ## §7.1 Excommunication Tribunal (ED-625 — approved 2026-04-17) [line 525] ... ## §10.1 Parliamentary Stay (ED-631) [line 548] ... ## §12 OPEN ITEMS AND EDITORIAL FLAGS [line 564]
verdict.reasoning: The underlying structural defect is real and verified in designs/scene/social_contest_v30.md. The section sequence (confirmed by grepping all ## §N headers) runs §1–§11, then §7.1 (line 525), then §10.1 (line 548), then §12 (line 564). So §7.1 and §10.1 are appended out of numeric order after §11, displaced ~200 lines from §7 (line 315) / §7.3 (line 367) where they are referenced. The cross-references the finding cites are genuine and located: §7.1's counter pointer at line 535 ("The correct strategic counter is preventing the filing... See §10.1 Parliamentary Stay. Filing can be prevented while CI < 55."), and the §7.3.2 closure-table row at line 391 ("If target's faction successfully passes a Parliamentary Stay (per §10.1) AND CI < 55: Investigation suspended for 1 season per Stay."). There is no table of contents and no internal anchor links in the file, so a GM truly does read linearly and meets these forward-references long before the sections resolve. That part holds. HOWEVER, a load-bearing specific in the claim is FALSE: it states §7.1/§10.1 are "physically placed after §12 (Open Items)" and that a reader hits the forward-references "after the doc has apparently ended at §12." The actual ordering is the reverse — §7.1 (525) and §10.1 (548) come BEFORE §12 (564). The document does not "apparently end at §12" before these sections appear; §12 is the genuine last major section and the displaced sections sit just ahead of it, wedged after §11 (Hybrid Contest). The real anomaly is out-of-numeric-sequence appendix placement after §11, not placement after §12. Because the finding's central explanatory mechanism ("after the doc has ended at §12") is refuted while the broader observation (out-of-sequence, hard to trace at the table) is sound, this is overstated rather than simply sound. Severity remains cosmetic — it is pure document ordering with zero mechanical consequence; the rules themselves cohere (the §7.3↔§10.1↔§7.1 loop is internally consistent: CI<55 gate, 1-season suspension, Stay-as-counter all agree across lines 391, 412, 535, 554, 560).
verdict.fix_assessment: Fix is engine-disciplined and correct in spirit: it is a pure documentation relocation (move §7.1 and §10.1 to sit with §7 asymmetric proceedings, ahead of §12), touching no resolver, Key bus, derived-stat bucket, or sigma/armature logic — no bolt-on, no mechanical change. One correction to the fix's own premise: it says relocate them "ahead of §12," which is already true (they precede §12); the actual repair is to move them into numeric sequence beside §7/§7.3 (i.e., place §7.1 after §7.3 and renumber/relocate §10.1 to follow §10, before §11), eliminating the after-§11 appendix wedge. With that framing the fix is sound.

findings.title: NPC Obligation priority-tree block is asserted by the contest spec but absent from every NPC priority tree
findings.claim: social_contest §6.1 promises that an NPC's loss has lasting strategic teeth: the priority tree is modified so any Obligation-violating action is blocked. But npc_behavior_v30 §8 — the standardized 7-level template and all nine faction trees (Church, Crown, Hafenmark, Varfell, Guilds, Löwenritter, RM, Edeyja, struck Niflhel) — contains no Obligation level, gate, or check. The only 'Obligation' references in the entire NPC doc are the Edeyja Wager extension. The hook the contest spec writes into does not exist in the consumer.
findings.impact: The headline AXIS-B promise — 'a won contest has lasting strategic impact — the player can constrain an NPC faction's behavior through political victory, not just stat changes' — is unhonored. An NPC contest loss produces a clock (§6.1) that nothing reads in the AI loop, so the NPC keeps acting freely and may immediately take the very action the Obligation forbids. The political consequence of beating an NPC evaporates; only the Mandate/Stability bump (itself contested, see B1-2) remains.
findings.fix: Add an Obligation gate to the §8.1 template as a Priority-1.5 rule (between Survival and Conviction-critical): 'If an active Obligation binds this faction, prune from the candidate action set any action whose effect would breach the Obligation's violation trigger, unless Stability ≤ 2 (Survival override).' This reuses the existing priority-tree primitive (candidate-set filtering) and the clock_registry Obligation clock as the data source — no new subsystem. Cross-reference it from social_contest §6.1.

locations.quote: the NPC priority tree is modified: any action that would violate the Obligation is blocked for the Obligation's duration unless the faction enters Survival priority (Stability ≤ 2)
| 1 | Survival | Universal | If Stability ≤ 2: take action that raises Stability or removes threat. Overrides all. |
| 4 | Maintain treaties. Defend held territory. Govern in lowest-Accord territory

verdict.evidence_quote: All NPC faction priority trees follow a standardised 7-level structure: | 1 | Survival | ... | 2 | Conviction-critical | ... | 3 | Framework-aligned opportunity | ... | 4 | Institutional Tendency | ... | 5 | Conviction-secondary | ... | 6 | Reactive | ... | 7 | Pass |
verdict.fix_assessment: Engine-disciplined and correct: it reuses the existing priority-tree primitive (candidate-set filtering at a named priority level) plus the already-registered clock_registry Obligation clock (§89, which carries source/parties/commitment/duration/violation-trigger) as the data source — no new subsystem. Two tightenings: (1) the new gate should test against the clock's own violation_trigger field rather than re-deriving 'would breach' logic, so breach semantics have one source of truth; (2) the Survival override (Stability ≤ 2) and duration should be read from clock state, not hardcoded a second time. Placement as Priority-1.5 (between Survival and Conviction-critical) is right because §6.1 already names Survival (Stability ≤ 2) as the sole carve-out. The fix should also be cross-referenced from npc_behavior §7.9 override conditions and the §8.1 template, and ideally the contest.py / faction sim should model it (both sims currently have zero Obligation enforcement).
verdict.reasoning: Verified at source on disk. social_contest_v30.md §6.1 line 286 makes the exact claim: 'When an NPC faction loses a contest and receives an Obligation, the NPC priority tree is modified: any action that would violate the Obligation is blocked for the Obligation's duration unless the faction enters Survival priority (Stability ≤ 2).' The consumer, npc_behavior_v30.md §8, is the AI loop: the §8.1 standardized 7-level template (Survival, Conviction-critical, Framework-aligned, Institutional Tendency, Conviction-secondary, Reactive, Pass) contains NO Obligation level, and I read all nine faction trees (§8.2 Church, §8.3 Crown, §8.4 Hafenmark, §8.5 Varfell, §8.6 Guilds, §8.7 Löwenritter, §8.8 Niflhel struck, §8.9 RM, §8.10 Edeyja) plus the §8.1 Accord extension, the §7.9 'Priority Tree Override Conditions' (only an Institutional Mandate trigger, no Obligation block), and §7.1-7.8 — none gate on Obligation. Grep for 'Obligation' across npc_behavior_v30.md returns only the Edeyja Wager extension (lines 655-664), exactly as the finding states. The data side exists but is inert here: clock_registry_v30.md §89 defines the Obligation clock carrying a 'violation trigger (what constitutes breach and fires the §6.1 consequences)' — it points the enforcement back at §6.1, which writes into a hook that does not exist in §8. Neither sim implements it: contest.py has zero 'Obligation' matches; tribunal.py's only match is the tribunal Evidence-Track clause, unrelated to priority-tree pruning. Not already-handled: the closest thing is the prior 2026-06-09 comprehensive audit, which catalogued this exact relationship as gate G-22 ('NPC Obligation block ... blocked unless Survival priority') and edge O-23 ('NPC strategy constraint ← NPC priority tree ← active Obligation') — but those are ASSERTED dependencies in an audit map, not an implementation in the priority-tree tables, which corroborates rather than refutes the gap. Severity corrected from critical to major: the AXIS-B headline promise (durable strategic constraint from a political win, not just stat changes) is genuinely unhonored, but this is a single dangling write-hook into an already-modeled data structure, fixable by one priority row, and the Mandate/Stability outcomes of a win still land — so it is severe but localized, not a corpus-wide failure.

findings.title: Contest 'Mandate +1/−1' writes are incoherent with the LPS-2e ruling that Mandate is a derived aggregate, not a writable stat
findings.claim: social_contest §6 Domain Echo (decisive+Memory → 'winning faction's Mandate +1') and §6.1 Obligation violations ('Mandate −1', '−2') write Mandate directly. But the 2026-05-30 Jordan ruling re-grained Mandate as a pure derived aggregate of per-settlement L/PS via Mandate = clamp(round(7·T/(T+K)),0,7); §1.8 explicitly states faction outcomes must 'apply their ΔL/ΔPS to the faction's controlled settlements ... which re-aggregate into Mandate — keeping per-settlement L/PS as canonical state and Mandate as the pure derived aggregate.' The contest spec still treats Mandate as a directly settable counter and specifies no L/PS application path.
findings.impact: There is no defined way to apply a contest's '+1 Mandate' under current canon. An engine following §1.8 cannot execute the contest spec literally; one following the contest spec writes a value that the next Accounting overwrites from settlement L/PS. The integration is ambiguous at exactly the contest→faction boundary, and the magnitude ('+1') is not even on the right scale (settlement ΔL/ΔPS are 0–7 per settlement, not a faction-level +1).
findings.fix: Restate the Domain Echo and Obligation consequences as ΔL/ΔPS applied uniformly to the winning/losing faction's controlled settlements (e.g. decisive Memory win → +1 L to each settlement in the cited precedent's domain, clamped 0–7, within the ±2/stat/season cap), letting the existing 7T/(T+6) aggregate produce the Mandate movement. This reuses the §1.8 mission-outcome path already defined for PP-686 outcomes — no new write channel.

locations.quote: Decisive win + Memory genre: winning faction's Mandate +1 in the domain of the cited precedent.

Faction-level mission outcomes ... now apply their ΔL/ΔPS to the faction's controlled settlements (uniformly, clamped 0–7), which re-aggregate into Mandate — keeping per-settlement L/PS as canonical state and Mandate as the pure derived aggregate.
any action that would violate the Obligation is blocked

verdict.evidence_quote: Faction-level mission outcomes (PP-686 cascade-fidelity / procedural / violation ΔL/ΔPS) now apply their ΔL/ΔPS to the faction's controlled settlements (uniformly, clamped 0–7), which re-aggregate into Mandate — keeping per-settlement L/PS as canonical state and Mandate as the pure derived aggregate.
verdict.reasoning: Both halves of the contradiction are verbatim and load-bearing. (1) The contest spec writes Mandate as a direct counter: social_contest_v30.md:230 "Decisive win + Memory genre: winning faction's Mandate +1 in the domain of the cited precedent"; :248-250 Obligation violations "Mandate −1 / −2"; the same direct writes are duplicated in params/contest.md:133. (2) The LPS-2e ruling re-grained Mandate as a pure derived aggregate: settlement_layer_v30.md:147 (§1.8 header naming "LPS-2e — Jordan ruling 2026-05-30"), :165-166 the formula Mandate = clamp(round(7·T/(T+K)),0,7) with K=6, and :175 (quoted above) mandating that faction-level outcomes route through ΔL/ΔPS on controlled settlements which re-aggregate into Mandate. The contest spec specifies NO L/PS application path, so an LPS-2e-compliant engine cannot execute "Mandate +1" literally, and a contest-spec-compliant engine writes a value the next Accounting overwrites from settlement L/PS (a derived_value write-conflict). I checked whether the gap is already-handled and it is NOT: §1.8 enumerates the covered mission-outcome sources as "PP-686 cascade-fidelity / procedural / violation" — social-contest Domain Echo / Obligation consequences are not named, and neither social_contest_v30.md nor params/contest*.md contain any "Mandate is derived / not written / apply ΔL/ΔPS" reconciliation (grep returned none). The scale objection in the finding is also correct: §1.8's settlement ΔL/ΔPS operate within a ±2 faction-stat seasonal cap and per-settlement ±1 (settlement_layer:173), so a flat faction-level "+1" is not even on the canonical write scale. Independent corroboration: the distillation_coherence_report.md:127 states the F1 invariant — "derived_value = recomputed aggregate, never written directly (F1)" — and lists "Mandate saturation" among confirmed dampers (:197), i.e. Mandate is canonically a derived_value; the contest's direct write violates F1. Note the contradiction is broader than the finding's three cited locations: the same defect recurs at social_contest:234/248-250/259/340/512/527/538-543 and pervasively across faction_state_authoring_v30.md (Censure/Embargo/Outlawry/Excommunication/treaty-breach all write Mandate ±N) — i.e. this is one instance of a corpus-wide write-channel debt LPS-2e created, but the finding's specific contest claim stands exactly as described. Severity major (not critical): it is a genuine integration defect at the contest→faction boundary that blocks literal engine execution, but it is a localized re-routing fix with a defined target path, not a system-breaking ambiguity, and is one of a known family the LPS structural-redesign ledger (_lps_structural_redesign_2026-05-30, cited at :177) already tracks for consumer rewiring.
verdict.fix_assessment: Engine-disciplined and correct. The fix reuses the §1.8 mission-outcome ΔL/ΔPS → re-aggregate path that already exists for PP-686 outcomes (no new write channel, no bolt-on), respects the existing 0–7 clamp and the ±2 faction-stat seasonal cap, and lets the canonical 7T/(T+6) aggregate produce the Mandate movement — i.e. it keeps Mandate as a pure derived_value per the F1 invariant. Two refinements to tighten it: (a) §1.8 currently enumerates only "PP-686 cascade-fidelity / procedural / violation" as routed sources, so the fix should also add social-contest Domain Echo / Obligation / BG-vote / Succession / Excommunication outcomes to that enumeration (or have the contest spec cite §1.8 as the application path) so the routing is explicit, not implied; (b) because §1.8 caps per-settlement change at ±1/season and ±2 faction-stat/season, the author must pick the ΔL magnitude that yields the intended ~1-step Mandate feel under the saturating aggregate — "+1 L to each settlement in the cited precedent's domain" is a reasonable default but should be sim-checked against the Stage-4 calibration rather than assumed to equal the old flat "+1 Mandate." The same restatement should be applied consistently to the other direct-Mandate writes in social_contest (§6.1, §6 Total Victory, §7.1, BG vote, §7.2 Succession) for a coherent fix.

findings.title: Contest→Mandate path is genuinely bounded — no win→Mandate→pool→win runaway
findings.claim: The runaway the brief asks me to suspect (a faction that keeps winning → more Mandate → bigger pool → wins more) is damped on every link. The per-contest Mandate gain is capped at ±2/stat/season (Domain Echo cap, throughlines PART 4 / scale_transitions §5), Mandate is hard-ceilinged at 7 (P-20), and the settlement→Mandate aggregate saturates via 7T/(T+6) with diminishing returns. The distillation report's loop inventory lists Mandate↔L/PS as a CONFIRMED-damper loop and does NOT list the contest→Mandate path among the six unconfirmed-damper risks.
findings.impact: AXIS-B boundedness for the Mandate channel holds. A faction stacking contest wins cannot spiral its political pool upward without limit; the saturating aggregate makes the marginal Mandate from each additional win shrink toward zero.

locations.quote: Loops with confirmed dampers — DO NOT TOUCH: Mandate↔L/PS (saturating `7T/(T+6)` + ±1 drift, Stage-4 sim-bounded)
the `T/(T+K)` form gives diminishing returns ... This is the Lesson-5 bound on the loop — `∂Mandate/∂q` shrinks as T grows, damping it.
Faction Mandate cannot exceed 7 (hard ceiling). All modifiers that would push above 7 are discarded.

verdict.evidence_quote: Cap: ±2 per stat per Echo. (ED-071 resolved by PP-329: one Echo/scene/faction prevents compounding)
verdict.fix_assessment: No fix needed and none proposed. The damping is already engine-disciplined and reuses existing primitives: contest→Mandate gains route through the Domain Echo channel hard-capped at ±2/stat/Echo with one Echo per scene per faction (scale_transitions §5.2), the settlement→faction Mandate aggregate saturates via the canonical 7T/(T+6) rollup (settlement_layer §1.8, same form as Treasury rollup), and P-20 applies a final accounting clamp at 7. No bolt-on. If anything the fix field could note the stronger fact uncovered below (no Mandate term in the personal-scale pool at all).
verdict.reasoning:
All three cited quotes verified verbatim: P-20 hard ceiling (strategic_layer_v30.md §159), the 7T/(T+6) diminishing-returns / Lesson-5 bound (settlement_layer_v30.md §1.8 lines 166-168), and the distillation report's confirmed-damper line listing "Mandate↔L/PS (saturating 7T/(T+6) + ±1 drift, Stage-4 sim-bounded)" (§7 line 139). The report's unconfirmed-damper risk list (lines 142-147) does NOT contain the contest→Mandate path — accurate. (It lists an npc_behavior↔social_contest opinion-oscillation 2-cycle, but that is a Disposition/opinion loop, not the Mandate-pool runaway.)

Tracing the actual pathway shows the boundedness holds and is in fact STRONGER than the finding states. The suspected runaway requires Mandate to feed the contest pool.
