verdict.reasoning: All three cited quotes verify verbatim against the working tree. (1) key_type_registry_v30.md §7 scene.contest_resolved (lines 664-680): emitting_systems: [social_contest], consuming_systems: [npc_behavior, faction_layer, articulation] — exact match. (2) references/module_contracts.yaml records the emit on BOTH sides: the social_contest module block (lines 431-432) emits scene.contest_resolved (terminal: false), and the consumers genuinely consume it — faction_layer/faction_state consumes it at line 72, npc_behavior at line 121, conviction_track consumes sibling scene.* keys. The closure is real and bidirectional, not just a one-sided registry assertion. (3) social_contest_v30.md §4 Step 6 (line 194) carries the ED-892 channel-reservation note verbatim. The channel-reservation invariant is independently corroborated in params/core.md: the "Pool floor: 1D" section (lines 224-228) explicitly lists "Spent + Rattled combined penalties in Contest" as subtractive POOL penalties (max(1, base_pool − penalties)) — confirming Rattled routes to −1D Pool, not +Ob, exactly as the finding claims. The finding's framing — that this is the one clean keyed contest output and the debt is the unkeyed post-resolution seam — is corroborated by the cross-referenced distillation_coherence_report.md (§3/§5), which independently states the contest is already on the Instance-A sigma kernel and the remaining work is pure "unification and seam-closure," not mechanic redesign. One minor nuance that does NOT undermine the finding: sim/personal/contest.py models the pipeline math (Argue Pool, Persuasion Track, Contest Fatigue, the 1D pool floor) but does not literally emit scene.contest_resolved — so "replay shape proven" is true at the spec/registry/contracts tier, not at the sim tier. This is consistent with the finding's own scope (it claims module_contracts records the emit, which it does) and is the normal division of labor in this corpus (sim verifies kernel math; contracts/registry carry the bus wiring). Severity cosmetic is honest: this is a strength/strength-pattern observation, not a defect.
verdict.fix_assessment: No fix is proposed and none is needed — the finding is a strength. Its forward-looking prescription (route the C1-1..C1-5 post-contest consequences onto this same Key or registered child Keys) is engine-disciplined: it reuses the existing Key bus and the proven scene.contest_resolved emitter/consumer wiring rather than bolting on a parallel channel, and it mirrors the pattern scene.battle_concluded already uses (officer deaths emit child state.standing_change Keys with cause = parent Key, per registry §7 line 702). That is the correct, no-new-primitive path. Recommend the eventual C1-1..C1-5 remediation cite the registry's existing child-Key-with-cause idiom explicitly.

strengths.title: The live track and pool-assembly are visibly surfaced — the player can see where they stand and why the pool is what it is
strengths.claim: The UI animates the contest score moving each exchange and shows the final position, and the Argue pool assembles on-screen with a per-source bonus breakdown before the roll, plus the Appraise audience-boost result and preparation/Findings bonuses are surfaced pre-commit. This is the opposite of a black box: the player watches the meter move toward or away from their win line and sees exactly which dice they earned.
strengths.impact: A player can distinguish a winning line from a losing one in real time (track direction) and can plan style choices against a transparent pool — directly mitigating the owner's stated opacity pain.

locations.quote: CLASH: swords cross, clang audio, Conviction Track jerks toward winner
Appraise — both orators roll Attunement, results compare, audience boost revealed or confirmed
Success (2) | Full boost identified (e.g. "this audience favours Revealing")

verdict.evidence_quote: Base pool:   Charisma 5 × 2  =  10D ... + Audience:  Revealing match  =  14D ... + Finding:   Church records   =  16D ... + MS target: Consequence      =  17D
verdict.reasoning: All three cited quotes are verbatim and correctly located: valoria_ui_ux_v4.md:1115 ("CLASH: swords cross, clang audio, Conviction Track jerks toward winner"), :1068 ("Appraise — both orators roll Attunement, results compare, audience boost revealed or confirmed"), and social_contest_v30.md:131 ("Success (2) | Full boost identified (e.g. \"this audience favours Revealing\")"). The source document is authoritative: v4.md Status reads "CANONICAL — approved for development reference," and its successor v4.1 explicitly states "v4 remains authoritative for content-level UI specification until incremental restatement completes," so citing v4 for content-level UI behavior is correct, not stale. The two load-bearing mechanical claims are independently corroborated against the engine, not invented by the UI doc: (1) the live, moving track with a win line is the canonical Persuasion Track — social_contest_v30.md:218 ("Persuasion Track ≥ 7 = Side A wins; ≤ 3 = Side B wins; 4-6 = compromise"), :234 (Total Victory at ≥9/≤1), :154 ("Persuasion Track moves (margin − resistance) toward winner's position") — and sim/personal/contest.py models exactly this (PERSUASION_TOTAL_VICTORY=9, track clamped 0–10, signed track_movement toward winner each exchange). (2) The §6.3.1 pool-assembly breakdown maps one-to-one onto real, canonical contest pool sources: Appraise→audience boost (§4 Step 1 table), genre/orientation style, Corroborate (PP-235), Finding citation, and Resonant Style targeting (+1D) — every line item the UI animates is a documented pool contributor. So the finding's core assertion (the player watches the meter move toward/away from a win line and sees exactly which dice they earned, pre-commit) is mechanically grounded. The one real blemish is terminology drift: the canonical primitive is the "Persuasion Track" everywhere in spec/params/sim, but v4.md consistently renames it "Conviction Track" (lines 787, 1018, 1074, 1115). This is a genuine editorial inconsistency, but it does not weaken the strength — the surfaced, moving track exists regardless of label — and it belongs in a separate naming-repair finding, not this one. Severity cosmetic is correct for a verified strength.
verdict.fix_assessment: No fix is proposed, and none is needed — this is a strength, not a defect. The reuse it documents IS engine-disciplined: the UI surfaces the existing Persuasion Track resolver output and the canonical pool-assembly bucket list rather than inventing a parallel display mechanic, so it is bolt-on-free by construction. The only adjacent action worth noting (out of scope for D1-2) is reconciling the "Conviction Track" vs "Persuasion Track" label so the UI spec names the same primitive the spec/params/sim name.

strengths.title: Hidden-ledger boundaries are deliberate fog-of-war with discovery paths, not opacity debt
strengths.claim: The genuinely hidden items — exact opponent/judge Resonant Style, NPC Scar count, audience boost before Appraise — each have an in-fiction discovery move (Appraise degrees) and graduated player-facing signals. The Scar produces a narrative tell ("Something shifted in [NPC]'s expression") and a momentary portrait crack without revealing the count, and the count becomes Appraise-revealable later. This is tension-generating concealment a player can act on, distinct from numbers the player simply cannot get.
strengths.impact: Preserves stakes/mystery (you should not see the NPC's exact Scar count) while keeping the path to knowledge legible (Appraise harder, build the relationship) — the correct side of the fog-of-war vs opacity line.

locations.quote: This is not a stat reveal — the player does not learn the Scar count. They learn that their words had structural impact.
Authority | Invoke specific authority figure or text the NPC's framework recognises as binding.

verdict.evidence_quote: When a player achieves Overwhelming on the Appraise step (Attunement, TN 7, Ob 1, net ≥ 3): GM reveals the NPC's primary Resonant Style in addition to one Belief (per existing social_contest_v30.md rules).
verdict.reasoning:
The finding's core thesis (these hidden items are deliberate fog-of-war WITH discovery moves, not opacity debt) is directionally correct, but it overstates how complete/specified two of its three legs are, and one cited quote is mischaracterized.

LEG 1 — opponent/judge Resonant Style via Appraise: SUPPORTED, but the mechanic lives ONLY in the infill, not the spec the finding cites. The actual discovery rule is at designs/npcs/npc_behavior_v30_infill.md §6.1 (line 110): "When a player achieves Overwhelming on the Appraise step ... GM reveals the NPC's primary Resonant Style in addition to one Belief" and (line 111) net ≥ 4 reveals secondary too. Critically, the corresponding §6.1 'Appraise Revelation' header in the SPEC file (designs/npcs/npc_behavior_v30.md lines 679-681) is an EMPTY STUB — heading followed by blank lines, then §6.1b. So social_contest §6.2's pointer "revealed per npc_behavior_v30 §6.1 (Appraise Revelation)" resolves to an empty section in the spec; only the infill carries content. That is a real co-file/atomization hygiene wrinkle the finding does not flag.

LEG 2 — audience boost before Appraise: SUPPORTED, and the strongest leg. social_contest_v30.md §4 Step 1 (lines 127-132) gives a graduated ladder: Failure(0)=misleading signal (GM names a WRONG boost), Partial(1)=axis type only, Success(2)=full boost, Overwhelming(3+)=boost + one detail (a Belief, resistance threshold, or emotional state). Textbook fog-of-war with an in-fiction discovery move.

LEG 3 — NPC Scar count "becomes Appraise-revealable later": NOT SUPPORTED at the mechanical level — this is the finding's overstatement. social_contest §6.2 (line 292) promises "If they Appraise in a future contest, the Scar count is revealed per npc_behavior_v30 §6.1." But §6.1 (both empty in spec AND, in the infill, line 110) reveals RESONANT STYLE + one Belief — it never mentions Scar COUNT. A grep across designs/ + params/ for any Appraise->Scar-count rule returns nothing; npc_behavior line 387 confirms the Contest Appraise Overwhelming reveals "one Belief," not Scar count. The cited "conviction_track_v1.md §2" (actual path designs/personal/conviction_track_v1.md) specifies per-Conviction Scar ACCUMULATION/tracking, not Appraise revelation of the count. So the discovery path the finding presents as legible-but-deferred is in fact a DANGLING cross-reference — the narrative tell ("Something shifted in [NPC]'s expression") and momentary portrait crack are genuinely specified (§6.2 lines 290-292), but the promised later Appraise reveal of the count is unwritten.

QUOTE MISCITE: the finding's second location quotes npc_behavior §6.3 "Targeting Effects" (the Authority row) as if it were a discovery move. The quote is verbatim-accurate (line 700) but §6.3 is the TARGETING-EFFECT table — what happens mechanically when you target a known style (+1D, Doubt Marker) — not how a player DISCOVERS the hidden style. It does not support the "in-fiction discovery move" claim; the actual discovery move is the Appraise ladder.

Net: the finding correctly identifies that the audience-boost and (via infill) Resonant-Style items sit on the right side of the fog-of-war/opacity line. But it presents the Scar-count discovery path as established when it is a dangling promise, and mis-cites a targeting-effects table as a discovery mechanism. The strength claim holds for 2 of 3 legs; the third is aspirational. Severity stays cosmetic (it's a "strength" finding and the spirit is right), but the verdict is overstated because the corpus does not yet deliver the Scar-count discovery path it asserts.
verdict.fix_assessment: The finding's fix is empty (it is a strength). If the implied design intent is to be honored, the engine-disciplined move is to fill the empty spec-file §6.1 'Appraise Revelation' stub (designs/npcs/npc_behavior_v30.md line 679) to actually specify a Scar-count revelation tier on the EXISTING Appraise net-success ladder — e.g., extend the §4 Step 1 table / §6.1 so a sufficiently high Appraise net reveals Scar count, reusing the sigma resolver's net-success buckets rather than adding a new roll. That keeps the social_contest §6.2 pointer honest and reuses the existing Appraise primitive (no bolt-on). The mis-cited §6.3 location should be replaced with the §4 Step 1 Appraise table (lines 127-132) and infill §6.1 as the true discovery-move anchors.
